title: Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states
full_text: Other Editions 29 April 2026 Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states Australian Broadcasting Corporation News national work reporter Bronwyn Herbert and the Specialist Reporting Team's Melanie Vujkovic 28 April 2026 1245 words English ABCNEW (c) 2026 Australian Broadcasting Corporation Australia risks repeating the mistakes it made with social media unless it moves quickly to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace, according to a new report. The study, produced by the John Curtin Research Centre and backed by the SDA, Australia's largest retail and fast-food union, warns unchecked AI could intensify worker surveillance, unsafe workloads and job insecurity. Drawing comparisons to social media, where the absence of early rules allowed platforms to become conduits for misinformation and public harm, researchers have called on the federal government to ensure proper AI guardrails are in place before the spread becomes unmanageable. "AI is so much more powerful than social media," co-author of the report Dominic Meagher said. "We do not have the luxury of getting it wrong this time." Dr Meagher said the report was not anti-innovation but warned there needed to be clear regulations laid out in a national strategy, with workers at the centre. "Just because AI makes a decision, it doesn't mean that it's an excuse for the company to sidestep their obligations [to worker's rights]," he said. "Companies, where they are working with their workforce, where it's actually integrating it in their workflow, those companies are able to turn AI adoption into more profit." On Tuesday, Workplace Relations Minister Amanda Rishworth told the AFR Workforce Summit the Albanese government had conducted a workforce "gap analysis" into the effects of AI on jobs. Preliminary results cited by the federal government indicate AI has slowed growth in some occupations such as filing clerks and keyboard operators, but the mix of jobs in the economy has not changed faster than usual. Employment outcomes for young tertiary graduates have been positive, the results suggest, countering fears they would be early casualties of AI. The government is also developing the capability to monitor the impacts of AI by analysing changes in the labour market since ChatGPT's launch in November 2022, with a focus on entry level jobs and workforce composition. Australia's 'complex' legal framework However, the John Curtin Research Centre report is calling for a national AI taskforce; a review of the Fair Work Act to address AI related workplace risks; and to require mandatory human oversight of AI at work. A key recommendation is to establish an AI expert advisory panel within the Fair Work Commission to help assess AI-related disputes and ensure existing workplace protections continued to apply. The researchers said employers must consult with workers and unions before AI was deployed and there needed to be universal access to AI education and skills. "Australia's spent a long time building a pretty fair place and we shouldn't just sidestep it because new technology comes along," Dr Meagher said. Workplace relations and safety lawyer Shannon Chapman said there was no national, overarching piece of legislation that deals with AI in the workplace. "If someone comes to me and asks for advice about implementing biometrics data scanners in the workplace, that's not necessarily a quick or easy answer. It will be jurisdiction specific," she said. "It will depend on the type of data that's being gathered, how it's going to be stored, how it might be able to be used … it's a complex legal framework." There are also several federal laws to consider, including anti-discrimination, human rights, and the Fair Work Act. Ms Chapman said consistency around workplace surveillance laws across states and territories would be helpful, but overarching legislation could also have the potential to add another layer of legal complexities. New AI-related employee rights would not be simple — it could complicate the legal landscape and create practical challenges for employers to follow the rules. "For example, around job security or restrictions on using AI to monitor and track work, then there would be a question there as to how that interacts with existing rights and entitlements and also employer compliance obligations," Ms Chapman said. In her speech, Ms Rishworth said a forum made up of government, employers and unions would meet for the first time today, to examine the "key themes" in adoption of AI in workplaces: trust, capability, transparency, safety and productivity. "These themes will shape our discussions on how we can build common understanding, and translate these themes into agreed outcomes," she said. What technology is available right now? Notion Digital Forensics managing director Matt O'Kane said most employers were already monitoring their staff to some extent for cybersecurity or cyber insurance purposes. "Microsoft Office 365 might be monitoring actions that you might do," he said. "In general, I see them used after an incident, which most Australians would think that's reasonable if something's happened in the workplace, we need to look into it, no problem." But Mr O'Kane said more "intrusive" international tools were being adopted in Australia that could monitor things like on-screen activity and keystrokes. He said there have also been reported incidents through Fair Work where a company has gone "over the line", turning staff laptops into covert listening devices while they worked from home. "There's a huge trend with technology vendors to introduce AI, and it's easy to see why — we've all tried it, we understand that it can boost our productivity," he said. "But when they introduce AI, we run a risk. Would we want this to happen if this was managed by a person, would we want a person to monitor this? "So we want to be careful that we're not importing technology from overseas where there's different workplace culture, workplace expectations, and bringing it to Australia … without really thinking through [if it's] a reasonable deployment." 'The biggest change of all' Mr O'Kane said for now, humans were still in charge of what an AI tool did, but employers needed to make sure they did not skip properly testing and understanding it. "There's a limit to human monitoring but there's typically no limits to AI monitoring," he said. "We need a safe workplace, but we want a friendly workplace, a trustworthy workplace. "Just because it's AI, it doesn't remove your personal responsibility. You need to test that AI to make sure it operates in your name in a way that you're happy with as an employer." Ms Chapman said it was important for employers to be at the forefront of policies and help ensure they were regularly reviewed. "It's really critical from my perspective as someone advising clients to have really fundamental underpinning policies and procedures and terms in your contract of employment that deal with use of AI and what is and is not appropriate," she said. "'Do you have a policy about AI use? What does it say? Does it clearly tell your employees what the consequences are if they breach that? Have you trained your employees on it?'" Dr Meagher said the scale of AI's impact on the way we work could not be under-estimated. "In a sense, it's verging on discovering alien life, but we've discovered it in our own computers," he said. "AI is going to be the biggest change of all and we really need to make sure it lifts up everyone." summary: A report by the John Curtin Research Centre warns that Australia lacks a national strategy to regulate the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in the workplace, potentially leading to increased worker surveillance and job insecurity. The report calls for the establishment of a national AI taskforce and mandatory human oversight of AI systems. It highlights the need for clear regulations to protect workers' rights and ensure responsible AI deployment.
publication:
Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states
Australian Broadcasting Corporation News28 April 20261,245 wordsnational work reporter Bronwyn Herb

Australia risks repeating the mistakes it made with social media unless it moves quickly to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace, according to a new report.
key_points: The report compares the current AI landscape to the unregulated rise of social media, urging prompt government action.
Recommendations include a review of the Fair Work Act and the creation of an AI expert advisory panel within the Fair Work Commission.
Workplace Relations Minister Amanda Rishworth indicated the government is analysing AI's impact on jobs and engaging stakeholders in discussions about AI adoption.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights emerging regulatory challenges and the need for compliance in the evolving landscape of workplace technology.
